content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Harbor is a fully open-source container registry, while JFrog is a proprietary/commercial platform that offers both self-hosted and cloud-hosted solutions. “Cloud Registries” (like AWS ECR or Google Artifact Registry) are proprietary, managed services.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

open source registry1. Harbor

• Type: Open Source (CNCF Graduated Project)

• Hosting: Self-hosted (you deploy it on your own servers or Kubernetes clusters).

• Best for: Organizations that need an enterprise-grade private registry with strict data sovereignty, air-gapped environments, and built-in security features like vulnerability scanning.

• Website: Harbor Project
2. JFrog (Artifactory)

• Type: Proprietary / Commercial

• Hosting: Available as a self-hosted platform or as a fully managed SaaS (Cloud) solution.

• Best for: Universal artifact management (storing not just Docker images, but also npm, Maven, and Python packages) and teams deeply integrated with CI/CD pipelines.

• Website: JFrog Artifactory
3. Cloud Registries (AWS ECR, Google Artifact Registry, Azure ACR)

• Type: Proprietary

• Hosting: Managed Cloud Services

• Best for: Teams who want to avoid managing backend infrastructure and prefer a registry integrated directly with their specific cloud provider’s permissions and deployment ecosystem.
Open Source Registry
1. Open Source Registry — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
A registry is a place where container images are stored.
Think of it like GitHub, but instead of source code, it stores Docker images.
Examples:

• Docker Hub (public)

• Harbor (open source)

• JFrog Artifactory

• AWS ECR

• Azure ACR

• Google Artifact Registry
Example:
docker pull nginx:latestDocker downloads the image from a registry.
2. Secures Artifacts — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
Artifact
An artifact is any software package produced after building your application.
Examples include:

• Docker images

• JAR files

• WAR files

• ZIP files

• Helm charts

• npm packages

• Python wheels
Example:
Angular App
│
▼
Build
│
▼
Docker Image
│
▼
ArtifactSo secure artifacts means protecting these files from tampering or unauthorized access.
3. Policies — — — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
Policies are rules that determine what users can and cannot do.
Examples:
Policy 1
Only DevOps can push images.Policy 2
Developers can pull images only.Policy 3
Reject images larger than 2 GB.Policy 4
Do not allow images with critical vulnerabilities.Policies help automate security and governance.
4. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) — — — — — — — — — — — — — —
RBAC stands for Role-Based Access Control.
Instead of giving permissions to every individual, you assign permissions to roles.
Example:
| User | Role | Permission |
| ------- | ------------- | ------------- |
| Alice | Developer | Pull Images |
| Bob | DevOps | Push & Delete |
| Charlie | Security | Scan Images |
| Admin | Administrator | Everything |Without RBAC:
Alice
↓
100 permissions manuallyWith RBAC:
Developer Role
│
├── Pull
├── View
└── Read LogsAlice
│
Assigned Developer Role
This is much easier to manage.
5. Images are Scanned
Before an image is used, it is scanned for known security issues.
Example Docker image:
FROM ubuntu:20.04Suppose Ubuntu has a known vulnerability.
The scanner reports:
Critical Vulnerability
OpenSSL
CVE-2025-12345
Severity: CriticalCommon scanners include:

• Trivy

• Clair

• Grype

• Snyk
Harbor can automatically scan every uploaded image.
Workflow:
Developer Pushes Image
│
▼
Registry
│
▼
Security Scan
│
▼
Report Generated6. Vulnerabilities
A vulnerability is a security weakness that attackers can exploit.
Examples:

• Old OpenSSL version

• Log4Shell

• Heartbleed

• Weak Linux package
Example:
Ubuntu Image
├── OpenSSL 1.0
├── curl
├── bashIf OpenSSL has a known issue:
Image
└── Vulnerability FoundThe scanner detects this before deployment.
7. Free from Vulnerabilities
This means the registry enforces rules such as:
If Critical Vulnerability Exists
❌ Reject Imageor
Only allow images with
High = 0
Critical = 0So vulnerable images never reach production.
8. Signs Images
Image signing proves:

• who created the image

• that it hasn’t been modified

• that it came from a trusted source
Think of it like digitally signing a PDF.
Without signing:
Docker Image
???
Who created it?With signing:
Docker Image
│
Digital Signature
│
VerifiedPopular tools:

• Cosign (Sigstore)

• Notary

• Docker Content Trust
9. Trusted Images
A trusted image is one that has been:

• built by your CI/CD pipeline

• scanned

• approved

• digitally signed
Example pipeline:
Developer
│
▼
GitHub
│
▼
CI Pipeline
│
▼
Docker Build
│
▼
Scan
│
▼
Sign
│
▼
Registry
│
▼
ProductionProduction servers can be configured to run only signed images, rejecting anything unsigned.
Putting it all together
Imagine a developer pushes a Docker image.
Developer
│
▼
Push Image
│
▼
Harbor Registry
│
├───────────────┐
│ │
▼ ▼
RBAC Check Security Policies
│ │
▼ ▼
Allowed? Scan Image
│ │
│ Vulnerabilities?
│ │
│ Yes → Reject
│
▼
Sign Image
│
▼
Store in Registry
│
▼
Production Pulls Only Signed Images“Open source registry that secures artifacts with policies and role-based access control, ensures images are scanned and free from vulnerabilities, and signs images as trusted.”
In simple terms:

• It stores your Docker images and other software packages (artifacts).

• It controls who can access them using RBAC.

• It enforces security policies before accepting images.

• It automatically scans images for known vulnerabilities.

• It can block vulnerable images from being used.

• It digitally signs approved images so deployment systems know they are authentic and untampered.

• It helps ensure that only trusted, verified images are deployed into development, testing, or production environments.
This combination of access control, automated scanning, policy enforcement, and image signing is why enterprise registries such as Harbor are commonly used instead of relying solely on a public registry like Docker Hub.
